coredata.dc:title: A fluorescence aptasensor based on carbon quantum dots and magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles for highly sensitive detection of 17β-estradiol
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Qingyi
Zhang, Peiyao
Pu, Hongbin
Sun, Da-Wen
coredata.dc:description: Trace amounts of 17β-estradiol (E2) in food and the environment poses a threat to human health, which has created the demand for sensitive analytical methods to detect E2. In this study, a novel fluorescent aptasensor was developed for sensitive detection of E2 based on double-chain hybridization between carbon quantum dots-labelled with E2 aptamer (CQDs-aptamer) and Fe3O4 nanoparticles modified by complementary DNA (Fe3O4-cDNA). Under the optimal conditions, the aptasensor displayed a good linear range of 10-11–10-6 M for E2 with the coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.996, and a low detection limit of 3.48 × 10-12 M was obtained. Besides, the aptasensor showed high selectivity and good reproducibility for E2 detection, which was successfully applied to the sensitive detection of E2 in milk as compared with tap water and lake water with satisfactory recoveries from 85.21% to 114.80%, suggesting the great significance of this aptasensor for detecting food contaminants in the food industry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Magnetic separation
Aptamer
17β -Estradiol
Milk
Water

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of CQDs and Fe3O4
Synthesis and characterization of CQDs-aptamer and Fe3O4-cDNA
Fluorescence detection of E2 in standard solutions
Fluorescence detection of E2 in real samples
Feasibility analysis and optimization of detection conditions for detecting E2
Sensitivity of aptasensor for E2 detection
Specificity and reproducibility for E2 detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the proposed aptasensor (A) and its actual operation diagram for E2 detection (B).

(A) TEM image, (B) UV–vis absorption (Abs), excitation (EX) and emission (EM) spectra and (C) FT-IR spectra of CQDs, insets of (A) and (B) are particle size distribution histogram and optical pictures (left is under daylight and right is under 365 nm ultra-violet light) of CQDs, respectively; (D) SEM image of prepared Fe3O4, the inset are photographs of magnetic properties of prepared Fe3O4; (E) FT-IR spectra of standard Fe3O4 powder (a) and prepared amine-functionalized Fe3O4 (b); (F) UV–vis absorption and (G) FT-IR spectra of CQDs and CQDs-aptamer; (H) UV–vis absorption and (I) XPS spectra of Fe3O4 (a) and Fe3O4-cDNA (b), inset of (I) is high-resolution XPS spectra of P 2p. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs-aptamer (a), CQDs-aptamer/Fe3O4-cDNA (b) and E2/CQDs-aptamer/Fe3O4-cDNA (c) with magnetic separation; (B) Fluorescence intensities and (C) relative fluorescence intensities (F-F0) of the aptasensor when CQDs-aptamer was incubated with E2 for different times (Blank is the sample without E2). (D) Fluorescence intensities of CQDs-aptamer with and without E2 under different hybridization times with Fe3O4-cDNA. (E) F-F0 of aptasensor under different hybridization times with Fe3O4-cDNA. F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities of aptasensor with 10-6 M E2 and without E2. Different letters represent statistically significant differences (P < 0.05) among these conditions.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of aptasensor with E2 concentrations ranging from 1 × 10−12 to 1 × 10−6 M; (B) Linear plot between F-F0 of aptasensor and the logarithm values of E2 concentrations ranging from 1 × 10−11 to 1 × 10−6 M; (C) The fluorescent intensities of aptasensor with 10-6 M interferences and 10-7 M E2, and different letters represent statistically significant differences (P < 0.05) among E2 and interferences; (D) F-F0 of 10 parallel samples with 10-7 M E2.
Comparison of the present aptasensor with other reported sensors for E2 detection.

row.entry: Tap water, environmental water, urine, milk
Fluorescent sensor based on magnetic molecularly imprinted polymers
Fluorescent aptasensor using a quantum dot-bioconjugate as a nano-bioprobe
Tap water, bottled water and Qinghe wastewater plant
Microfluidic chip aptasensor based on magnetic tripartite DNA assembly nanostructure probes
Colorimetric sensor based on gold nanoparticles and shortening DNA aptamers
Fishpond water, lake water and tap water
Electrochemical sensor using electropolymerized molecularly imprinted polymer and gold nanomaterials
Fluorescent aptasensor based on CQDs and Fe3O4
Milk, tap water and lake water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recoveries of E2 in real samples based on this aptasensor.

head.ce:title: A fluorescence aptasensor based on carbon quantum dots and magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles for highly sensitive detection of 17β-estradiol

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: ITMO University, Lomonosova Street 9, Saint-Petersburg 191002, Russia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: ITMO University, Lomonosova Street 9, Saint-Petersburg 191002, Russia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Trace amounts of 17β-estradiol (E2) in food and the environment poses a threat to human health, which has created the demand for sensitive analytical methods to detect E2. In this study, a novel fluorescent aptasensor was developed for sensitive detection of E2 based on double-chain hybridization between carbon quantum dots-labelled with E2 aptamer (CQDs-aptamer) and Fe3O4 nanoparticles modified by complementary DNA (Fe3O4-cDNA). Under the optimal conditions, the aptasensor displayed a good linear range of 10-11–10-6 M for E2 with the coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.996, and a low detection limit of 3.48 × 10-12 M was obtained. Besides, the aptasensor showed high selectivity and good reproducibility for E2 detection, which was successfully applied to the sensitive detection of E2 in milk as compared with tap water and lake water with satisfactory recoveries from 85.21% to 114.80%, suggesting the great significance of this aptasensor for detecting food contaminants in the food industry.

ce:section.ce:para: 17β-estradiol (E2) is a natural estrogen with high biological activity secreted by the gonads, which plays a very important role in promoting the growth and development of women (Adeel et al., 2017). E2 can also raise the milk yield of animals by improving their lactation ability, so it is often illegally used in animal husbandry, which leads to a larger number of residues of E2 in milk and other dairy products (Wilson et al., 2002). The E2 concentration in commercial whole milk was ranged from 4 to 14 ng/mL according to the previous report (Wolford and Argoudelis, 1979). In addition, E2 in the excretion metabolites of organisms and the industrial wastewater from the production of estrogen drugs is finally discharged into the water environment through sewage and runoff (Pu et al., 2019b), and an investigation showed that the E2 concentration in groundwater of farmland using livestock fertilizer was between 6 ∼ 66 ng/L (Peterson et al., 2000). However, studies proved that a series of diseases including endocrine disorders, reproductive dysfunction and tumours can be caused when humans are exposed to excessive amounts of E2 from food and the environment (Li et al., 2015), and even very low concentrations (ng/L) of E2 can have a toxic effect on organisms (Routledge et al., 1998). So it is imperative to develop highly sensitive, rapid and accurate methods for the detection of E2 in food and the environment.

At present, traditional detection methods of E2 such as high-performance liquid chromatography (Shi et al., 2011), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (Matějíček and Kubáň, 2008) and gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (Sghaier et al., 2017) have the advantages of high sensitivity and accuracy (He et al., 2020; Hussain et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2020a). Nevertheless, in these methods, it is necessary to separate and concentrate E2 before detection due to the complexity of the actual samples and trace or ultra-trace residues of E2, and thus complicated pretreatment process and a large amount of organic solvents are needed (Pu et al., 2019b). Furthermore, the analysis of E2 often requires derivatization because of its polarity and low volatility, which is time-consuming and environmentally unfriendly (Wu et al., 2016). And these methods often require expensive instruments, which further limits their routine analysis in the laboratory (Hu et al., 2020a; Wang et al., 2020b). On the other side, lots of chemical and spectral methods are also applied in the detection of E2. The electrochemical method has inherent merits of high sensitivity and low cost, but the electrochemical activity of E2 is weak and the oxidation potential is relatively high during the detection process, which may cause the phenomenon of electrode passivation, resulting in poor specificity and reproducibility of the experiment (Hu et al., 2020b; Pu et al., 2019b). Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS), the technique of absorbing molecules on the rough surface of nano-metal materials (substrates) to enhance Raman signals of the molecules (Hu et al., 2021; Hussain et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2021), has been used to detect E2 quickly and sensitively (Pu et al., 2019c), while the stability and reproducibility of this method need to be improved because a slight change in the SERS substrates can cause differences in the results (Jiang et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2021b). Therefore, there is an urgent need for a method with better performance to detect E2 in a complex sample matrix.

Fluorescence methods have attracted much attention from researchers because of their high sensitivity, good reproducibility and ease of operation (Achatz et al., 2011). Generally, fluorescent elements used in fluorescence methods include fluorescent dyes, quantum dots and biological fluorophores (Sun et al.,2021), among which carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are fluorescent carbon nanomaterials with a diameter less than 10 nm and are rich in various functional groups on their surfaces (Wei et al., 2021). Studies showed that CQDs have been widely utilized to construct fluorescence sensors for harmful substances detection in the environment and food by virtue of their stable fluorescence performance, good biocompatibility, water solubility, low toxicity, easy synthesis and low production cost (Lim et al., 2015). However, other substances in the food matrix and the environment may interfere with the fluorescence detection of the target. In terms of improving the selectivity of fluorescence sensors, aptamers, which are a piece of single-stranded DNA or RNA, have been applied in fluorescence sensors due to their specific binding to the targets (Jayan et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2020). For example, Zhang et al. (2018) constructed a fluorescence aptasensor for highly selective detection of E2 based on carboxyfluorescein-labelled E2 aptamer and its partially complementary DNA modified with Black Hole Quencher 1, and the results showed that the aptasensor could accurately identify and detect E2 in the presence of other endocrine-disrupting chemicals. Furthermore, in order to simplify the experimental operation and quickly separate the targets from complex sample matrices, magnetic nanomaterials are also introduced into the fluorescence aptasensor (Zhang et al., 2021a). For instance, Li et al. (2018) established an aptasensor for simple and sensitive determination of bisphenol A using magnetic Fe3O4 and AHN-labeled aptamer (AHN, 4-N-aminoethyl-N-hydroxyethyl-1,8-naphthalimide, a fluorescent dye), and found that the magnetic separation process could also effectively reduce the interference of other chemicals.

Based on the excellent performance of CQDs and the urgency of E2 detection, this study aims to develop a novel fluorescence aptasensor by the combination of CQDs and magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles for the highly sensitive and specific detection of E2 in milk as compared with water samples. The principle of the constructed aptasensor is shown in Fig. 1 A. Initially, E2 aptamer was labelled by carboxy-rich CQDs through amide reaction, and the synthesized CQDs-aptamer was hybridized with complementary DNA (cDNA) that was modified on Fe3O4 in the same way, thus forming a complex of CQDs-aptamer/Fe3O4-cDNA. When E2 was present and incubated with CQDs-aptamer firstly, the specific binding between E2 and its aptamer prevented double-strand hybridization of aptamer and cDNA. For the actual operation process (Fig. 1B), the complex was formed in the absence of E2, which was then separated by a magnet and resulted in low fluorescence intensity (F0) of supernatant. However, the presence of E2 prevented the formation of the complex, inducing a higher fluorescence intensity (F) of supernatant after magnetic separation. Therefore, the concentration of E2 was quantitatively analyzed according to the increase in fluorescence intensity. Compared with using the target to dissociate the double-stranded structure formed by aptamer and cDNA and then combine with the aptamer (Hayat et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2018), it was easier for the target to combine directly with its aptamer in this novel aptasensor, thus producing a higher sensitivity. In addition, the successful detection of E2 in milk and environmental water samples demonstrated the potential of the aptasensor in practical applications.

Citric acid, ethylenediamine, iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), anhydrous sodium acetate, 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and 1,6-hexanediamine were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Bisphenol A (BPA), diethylstilbestrol (DES), estrone (E1) and trifluoroacetic acid were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Co. (Shanghai, China). 17β-Estradiol (E2) and estriol (E3) were bought from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethylene glycol, ethanol and methanol were acquired from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). The sequences of E2 aptamer and its complementary DNA (cDNA) were 5′-NH2-(CH2)6-GCT TCC AGC TTA TTG AAT TAC ACG CAG AGG GTA GCG GCT CTG CGC ATT CAA TTG CTG CGC GCT GAA GCG CGG AAG C-3′ and 5′-COOH-GCT TCC GCG CTT CAG CGC GCA GCA A-3′ (Zhang et al., 2018), which were supplied by Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The phosphate buffer (PB, pH 7.2, 10 mM) was prepared with KH2PO4 and Na2HPO4·12H2O. All reagents were analytically pure and no further purification was conducted. Ultrapure water purified by a Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore Co., Billerica, MA, USA) was used during the whole experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CQDs and Fe3O4
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs were prepared through a hydrothermal method with the assistance of a high throughput closed microwave digestion/extraction workstation (Jupiter-b, Sineo Microwave Chemistry Technology Co., Shanghai, China) as per our previous study (Wei et al., 2020a). Briefly, 2 g citric acid and 1.043 mL of ethylenediamine dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water were heated in the microwave workstation at 200 °C for 5 min. The obtained solution was firstly filtered with a 0.22 μm microporous membrane (Jinteng Experiment Equipment Co., Ltd., Tianjin, China), and then dialysed with a dialysis bag (1000 Da, Beijing Solarbio Science Technology Co., Ltd., Beijing, China) for 36 h to obtain purified CQDs solution, which was stored in a refrigerator (BCD-525WKPZM, Hefei Midea Refrigerator Co., Ltd., Hefei, China) at 4 °C for later use.

The amine-functionalized magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles were synthesized according to the one-pot method (Wang et al., 2006). The reactants of 1.0 g FeCl3·6H2O, 6.0 g 1,6-hexanediamine and 2 g anhydrous sodium acetate were first stirred vigorously with 30 mL of ethylene glycol at 50 °C for 1 h using a magnetic stirrer (MS7-H550Pro, DLAB Scientific Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). Then the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated in a drying oven (DHG-9240A, Yiheng Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) at 200 °C for 6 h. After that, the obtained black solution was washed three times with ethanol and ultrapure water, respectively, and vacuum dried (DZF-6050, Shanghai Qixin Science Equipment Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) at 50 °C for 6 h to obtain the amine-functionalized Fe3O4 power.

The morphologicalcharacteristics of CQDs and Fe3O4 were characterized by a transmission electron microscope (JEM-1400 Plus, JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) and a field emission scanning electron microscope (Zeiss Merlin FE-EM, Carl Zeiss NTS GmbH, Oberkochen, Germany), respectively. The UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra of CQDs were acquired by a spectrophotometer (UV-1800, Shimadzu Co., Kyoto, Japan) and an RF-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Kyoto, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of CQDs and Fe3O4 were obtained by a Tensor infrared spectrophotometer (Nicolet-iS50, Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Waltham, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CQDs-aptamer and Fe3O4-cDNA
ce:section.ce:para: The modifications of E2 aptamer on CQDs and cDNA on Fe3O4 were carried out by the classic carbodiimide method (Li et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2017) with several adjustments. Typically, 20 mg EDC and 30 mg NHS were added into 3 mL of CQDs under constant shaking for 30 min by a digital shaker (MS 3, IKA Inc., Staufen im Breisgau, Germany) to activate the carboxyl group of CQDs, followed by the addition of 10 μL of 100 μM aptamer and incubation (DKZ-2B, Shanghai Yiheng Instruments Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) at 37 °C with continuous shaking for 4 h. The obtained CQDs-aptamer solution was dialysed for 24 h to remove unattached aptamer and CQDs, which was then stored at 4 °C for later use. Similarly, 20 mg EDC and 30 mg NHS dissolved in 2 mL of PB buffer were mixed with 10 μL of 100 μM cDNA and oscillated for 30 min to activate the carboxyl group of cDNA, then 1 mL of 6 mg/mL Fe3O4 solution dissolved in PB buffer was added and incubated at the same conditions. The obtained Fe3O4-cDNA solution was washed with PB buffer and magnetically separated several times to remove unlinked cDNA, which was redissolved in 3 mL PB buffer and stored at 4 °C for further use.

The UV-1800 spectrophotometer was used to record the UV–Vis spectra of CQDs-aptamer and Fe3O4-cDNA. The FT-IR spectra of CQDs-aptamer were acquired by the Tensor infrared spectrophotometer. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of Fe3O4 and Fe3O4-cDNA were obtained by an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Axis Ultra DLD, Kratos Analytical Ltd., Manchester, UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of E2 in standard solutions
ce:section.ce:para: The E2 standard solution of 10-3 M was prepared in methanol and then diluted into different concentrations with ultrapure water. For E2 detection, an amount of 200 μL of CQDs-aptamer was incubated with 50 μL of E2 solutions of different concentrations (10-11-10-5 M) at 37 °C for 30 min. Subsequently, 250 μL of Fe3O4-cDNA was added under constant shaking at 37 °C for 75 min. After magnetic separation, the fluorescence spectra of the supernatant at 442 nm were measured with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of E2 in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Milk purchased from a local supermarket (Guangzhou, China) was used as the actual sample for E2 detection. An amount of 5 mL of milk was added to 20% trifluoroacetic acid and the pH was adjusted to 4.6, then the mixture was placed in a 45 °C water bath (HH-2, Changzhou Aohua Instrument Co., Ltd., Changzhou, China) for 10 min to precipitate protein in milk. Afterwards, the sample was centrifuged at 10000 rpm by a high-speed refrigerated centrifuge (Anhui Jiaven Equipment Industry Co., Ltd., Hefei, China) for 15 min at 10 °C and filtrated using the 0.22 μm microporous membrane. Then it was mixed with a 10 μM E2 stock solution to prepare the spiked solutions with E2 concentrations of 10, 100 and 1000 nM. The spiked experiment of the milk samples was then carried out according to the detection method of E2 in standard solutions, and the final spiked concentrations of E2 were 1, 10 and 100 nM. In order to further verify the practicality of the aptasensor, tap water from the laboratory and lake water from the Center Lake Park (Guangzhou, China) were also collected for E2 detection. Tap water was simply filtrated twice by the microporous membrane and the lake water was settling overnight at room temperature of 25 °C before the supernatant was filtered. The rest of the process was the same as that for treating the milk sample, and the detection results were compared with that of milk.

All fluorescence measurements were performed in triplicate and the mean values with their standard deviation were used as the final results. All data were processed and analyzed by Excel 2019 and Origin 2019b software (OriginLab Co., MA, USA). The particle sizes of CQDs in the TEM image and Fe3O4 in the SEM image were measured by Nano_Measurer 1.2 software (Department of Chemistry, Fudan University, China). In order to analyze significant differences (P < 0.05) among obtained data, one-way ANOVA with Duncan’s test was carried out using IBM SPSS Statistics 22 software (IBM Co., NY, USA). The fluorescence output signal was taken as F-F0, where F and F0 were fluorescence signals in the presence and absence of E2, respectively. Besides, the limit of detection (LOD) and the limit of quantitation (LOQ) of the aptasensor were calculated by 3S/k and 10S/k, where S is the standard deviation of the fluorescence intensity at 442 nm of the blank samples, and k represents the slope of the calibration curve (Hussain et al., 2020).

The morphological characteristics of CQDs were characterized by TEM image. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the CQDs synthesized by the rapid microwave-assisted hydrothermal method were nearly spherical and uniformly dispersed in water. The inset of Fig. 2A is the particle size distribution diagram of 80 randomly selected particles, which showed that the diameters of CQDs were between 2.0 and 6.0 nm with an average value of 3.53 nm. The optical properties of CQDs are shown in Fig. 2B, which indicated that the optimal fluorescence excitation (EX) and emission (EM) wavelengths of CQDs were located at 350 and 442 nm, and the UV–Vis spectra of CQDs had two absorption peaks at 239 and 346 nm due to π-π* transition of aromatic CC bonds (Gao et al., 2015) and n-π* transition of CO bonds (Yue et al., 2016), respectively. The illustration of Fig. 2B displays that the light yellow CQDs under natural light (left) emitted a bright blue fluorescence at an ultraviolet wavelength of 365 nm, indicating good fluorescence performance of the prepared CQDs. The results were basically consistent with our previous study (Wei et al., 2020b). Additionally, FT-IR spectra were used to explore the functional groups on the surface of CQDs. As shown in Fig. 2C, the strong wide peaks around 3250 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH bonds as reported by Feng et al. (2017). The peaks located at 1637, 1257, 1544 and 1378 cm−1 were caused by the stretching vibrations of CO, CN and the deformation vibrations of NH and –CH2, respectively (Liu et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2020; Zhai et al., 2014). In addition, the weak peaks due to the stretching vibrations of C-OH were observed at 1186, 1072 and 992 cm−1, which were consistent with the results reported by Liu et al. (2016). The results proved the presence of the amino, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CQDs, which also explained the good water-solubility of CQDs.

The SEM image of amine-functionalized Fe3O4 is shown in Fig. 2D. It can be seen that the Fe3O4 synthesized by the one-pot method had a nearly circular morphology with a particle size of about 50 nm, which was in line with some previous study (Wang et al., 2006). Moreover, the inset in Fig. 2D indicates the excellent magnetic properties of Fe3O4 by introducing an external magnetic field (the picture on the right). FT-IR spectra were also used to prove the successful connection of amine groups on Fe3O4. As seen from Fig. 2E, the prepared magnetic nanoparticles (curve b) also showed the stretching vibration of the Fe-O bond at 575 cm−1 (Bruce et al., 2004) compared with the FT-IR spectrum of standard Fe3O4 (curve a), proving the successful synthesis of Fe3O4. In addition, the new peaks at 3431 and 1626 cm−1 attributed to the stretching and deformation vibrations of the NH bond according to Wang et al. (2006) and Li et al. (2018), demonstrating the successful functionalization of the amine groups on Fe3O4.

The labelling of E2 aptamer on CQDs was characterized by UV–Vis and FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2F, the UV spectra of CQDs-labeled with aptamer displayed a new absorption peak at 260 nm, which belonged to purine and pyrimidine bases of the aptamer (Qi et al., 2018). The results of FT-IR spectra are shown in Fig. 2G. Compared with CQDs, two new peaks at 1226 and 1083 cm−1 appeared in the FT-IR spectra of CQDs-aptamer due to the symmetric and asymmetric vibrations of phosphate groups (Wang et al., 2019). Moreover, the peaks of guanine, thymine, adenine and cytosine nitrogenous bases located at 1709, 1644, 1570 and 1476 cm−1 according to Jangir et al. (2010) were also shown in CQDs-aptamer. All of these demonstrated the successful labelling of the aptamer on CQDs. On the other hand, the modification of cDNA on Fe3O4 was analyzed by UV–Vis absorption and XPS. Fig. 2H shows that there was no absorption peak in the UV–Vis spectra of Fe3O4 (curve a), and the absorption peak at 260 nm assigned to cDNA similarly appeared after the modification of cDNA on Fe3O4 (curve b). The results of elemental analysis using XPS in Fig. 2I indicated that the elements of Fe, O, N and C, which might be caused by carbon pollution, appeared in Fe3O4 (curve a) were also shown in Fe3O4-cDNA (curve b), while an additional peak of P element in phosphate group at 133.1 eV was observed in Fe3O4-cDNA, which was seen more clearly in the XPS P 2p shown in Fig. 2I. Similarly, Wang et al. (2015) also found the presence of P 2p signal located at 133.1 eV through comparative elemental analysis when conjugating amino-modified cDNA to the surface of hybrid CdTe quantum dots. Therefore, the above results proved the successful modification of cDNA on Fe3O4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility analysis and optimization of detection conditions for detecting E2
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the feasibility of the aptasensor, an E2 solution with a final concentration of 10-6 M was used for fluorescence detection. As seen in Fig. 3 A, the fluorescence spectrum of CQDs-aptamer (curve a) had the highest fluorescence intensity at 442 nm. After the solution was mixed with Fe3O4-cDNA, the fluorescence spectrum (curve b) of the supernatant showed an obvious decrease in fluorescence intensity because CQDs-aptamer was bound to Fe3O4-cDNA and was separated by the magnet. However, when E2 existed and first incubated with CQDs-aptamer, the specific binding of E2 to the aptamer prevented double-stranded hybridization, leading to higher fluorescence intensity (curve c) compared with curve b. The results showed that the aptasensor was feasible in E2 detection.

For obtaining the required detection performance, the incubation time of E2 and aptamer, as well as the hybridization time of aptamer and cDNA, was optimized. Fig. 3B shows the fluorescence intensities (F) of the aptasensor when CQDs-aptamer was incubated with E2 for different times (5, 15, 30, 45 and 60 min), then hybridized with Fe3O4-cDNA for 1 h, and the blank was the fluorescence intensity (F0) of aptasensor without E2. As can be seen from Fig. 3C, the relative fluorescence intensity (F-F0) of the samples increased significantly (P < 0.05) with the extension of incubation time and became stable at 30 min, indicating that the incubation of E2 and its aptamer reached saturation at this time. Compared with the process of E2 to untie the hybrid double-strand and then bind to the aptamer, the direct binding of E2 to the aptamer was obviously faster, which was completed in 30 min according to previous studies (Li et al., 2015; Long et al., 2014). Therefore, 30 min was used as the optimized incubation time for E2 and aptamer.

For optimizing the hybridization time between aptamer and cDNA, the sample group with E2 and the blank group without E2 were prepared for the experiments, where the sample group was obtained by incubating CQDs-aptamer with E2 for 30 min and then hybridizing with Fe3O4-cDNA for 15, 30, 45, 60, 75 and 90 min, respectively, while the blank group was prepared by directly hybridizing CQDs-aptamer with Fe3O4-cDNA for the same periods. Fig. 3D shows the changes of fluorescence intensities of the sample group and the blank group after magnetic separation. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of the blank group decreased more than that of the sample group with the extension of hybridization time, proving that the binding of E2 and aptamer led to more CQDs-aptamer in the supernatant. The relative fluorescence intensities of the two groups at different hybridization times are shown in Fig. 3E, which indicated that F-F0 reached its maximum in 75 min and was significantly different (P < 0.05) from the previous fluorescence intensities. Therefore, 75 min was selected as the optimal hybridization time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of aptasensor for E2 detection
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal detection conditions, the fluorescence spectra of E2 with different concentrations from 10-12 to 10-6 M detected by this aptasensor are shown in Fig. 4 A. The fluorescence intensity at 442 nm of the aptasensor gradually decreased with the decrease in E2 concentrations, which ascribed to that fewer CQDs-aptamer was bound to E2 and remained in the supernatant. When the concentration of E2 dropped to 10-12 M, the fluorescence could no longer be enhanced, indicating that the sensitivity of the aptasensor had reached the limit. Fig. 4B shows a broad linear relationship between F-F0 and the logarithm of E2 concentrations from 10-11 to 10-6 M, and the fitted equation was obtained as y = 28.55x + 337.61 with the coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.996. Based on this, the LOD and LOQ of E2 determined by this aptasensor were calculated to be 3.48 × 10-12 M and 1.16 × 10-11 M, respectively. The results suggested that the aptasensor had excellent sensitivity in quantitatively detectingE2 at trace levels.

Table 1 compares the effects of different methods for E2 detection. Ming et al. (2017) designed a fluorescence method to detect E2 in water samples based on magnetic molecularly imprinted polymers and obtained the LOD of 30 nM, but the process of molecular imprinting on the surface of Fe3O4 alone took more than 2 days. In addition, Long et al. (2014) constructed a fluorescent aptasensor for E2 detection using carboxyl quantum dots bioconjugate as a nano-bioprobe, but the aptasensor showed a narrow linear range of 0.82–20.5 nM and a LOD of 0.22 nM. In contrast, the currently established aptasensor had a wider range from 10-11 to 10-6 M and a lower detection limit of 3.48 pM based on the selection of excellent fluorophore CQDs and the design of target binding to the aptamer first, which made this aptasensor more suitable for the sensitive detection of trace E2 residues in real samples. It was also noteworthy that only 5 min was taken to prepare our CQDs with the assistance of microwave digestion, which was more time-saving and lower-cost compared to the direct purchase of fluorescence materials (Long et al., 2014; Ming et al., 2017; Yildirim et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2018). Moreover, the detection of E2 by standard chromatographic methods normally requires pre-processing steps such as extraction, purification and derivatization, and thus the detection is time-consuming and cumbersome. In contrast, the current aptasensor only needed simple pre-processing based on precipitation, centrifugation and filtration, providing rapidity and convenience on E2 detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and reproducibility for E2 detection
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to sensitivity, specificity and reproducibility are also crucial factors for the performance of the aptasensor. In order to explore the effect of E2 analogues and some ions that may coexist with E2 in the matrix on E2 detection, estrone (E1), estriol (E3), diethylstilbestrol (DES), bisphenol A (BPA) and ions including Ca2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Zn2+, SO4 2- and Cl- were selected as interfering substances to conduct experiments. As displayed in Fig. 4C, only E2 with a final concentration of 10-7 M could cause significant fluorescence enhancement (P < 0.05), while the interfering substances with a final concentration of 10-6 M could hardly cause the change of fluorescence intensity. The results were in line with the conclusions reported by Zhang et al. (2018) and He et al. (2020), which demonstrated that the aptasensor could detect E2 with good specificity. On the other hand, 10 parallel experimental samples containing E2 with a final concentration of 10-7 M were prepared for the reproducibility experiment, and the corresponding F-F0 at 442 nm is plotted in Fig. 4D, showing that the 10 groups of parallel samples all had enhancement in fluorescence intensity, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) of their F-F0 was calculated to be 3.98%, which was<5%, proving that the aptasensor had good repeatability on E2 detection.

Considering the possible existence of E2 in food, milk was selected to carry out the detection experiment of E2 in actual samples, which was then compared with water samples. After simple pretreatment and mixing with E2, the milk and water samples were incubated with CQDs-aptamer and then hybridized with Fe3O4-cDNA to obtain E2 spiked solutions with final concentrations of 1,10 and 100 nM, respectively, while the milk or water samples without spiking with E2 were used as controls for comparison. After magnetic separation, the fluorescence intensities of the supernatant were measured. Results in Table 2 demonstrate that no E2 was detected in the controls, the reason might be that the concentration of E2 in the controls was lower than the LOD of this aptasensor (He et al., 2020). Furthermore, the milk samples with different E2 spiked concentrations acquired good recoveries with a range of 85.21%-101.16%, and the RSDs obtained between 1.54% and 2.61% were all less than 5% (Ming et al., 2017), suggesting that the aptasensor was feasible to monitor E2 contents in food samples. In addition, the recoveries of E2 in the two water samples were from 92.80% to 114.80% with RSDs < 5%, indicating that the aptasensor based on CQDs and magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles could be an alternative approach for accurate and sensitive detection of E2 in real samples.

In conclusion, CQDs were firstly applied in a novel fluorescence aptasensor for highly sensitive and selective detection of trace E2 residues, and the introduction of magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles provided convenience and rapidity for experimental operation. By virtue of the specific binding of E2 to the aptamer, CQDs labelled by the aptamer were used as fluorescence probes for the identification and accurate quantification of E2. It is worth mentioning that the aptasensor only needed simple pretreatment such as precipitation and centrifugation to achieve the detection of E2 in milk and water samples. Further, owing to sensitivity, stability and reliability, the current aptasensor provides a promising route for the detection of other harmful residues in food samples by replacing different aptamers. Moreover, in view of the characteristics of magnetic nanoparticles, it can be considered to use aptamer-labelled magnetic nanomaterials to enrich and separate the target from the complex sample matrix, and then perform subsequent quantitative detection.

Qingyi Wei: Supervision, Validation, Funding acquisition, Resources. Peiyao Zhang: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Investigation. Hongbin Pu: Validation, Funding acquisition, Resources. Da-Wen Sun: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (3217161084) for its support. This research was also supported by the Guangzhou Key Laboratory for Intelligent Sensing and Quality Control of Agricultural Products (202102100009)), the Guangdong Provincial Science and Technology Plan Projects (2020A1414010160), the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2020A1515010936), the Contemporary International Collaborative Research Centre of Guangdong Province on Food Innovative Processing and Intelligent Control (2019A050519001) and the Common Technical Innovation Team of Guangdong Province on Preservation and Logistics of Agricultural Products (2021KJ145).
